text: This is the case of a right lower lobectomy.
You can see how the instruments can be used in both hands.
Using simple wrist movements, the Maryland dissector can be orientated to the appropriate angle.
The bipolar energy device is useful because there is less displacement of heat compared with monopolar, so you could coagulate small vessels.
The inferior pulmonary vein in this case was taken first as the fissure was incomplete.
You can see that the instruments can be easily changed, for example, to a sucker or a standard diathermy hook because the surgeon is at the bedside and doesn't rely on their assistant.
You can also see how precisely the Marylands can be controlled to follow the tissue planes around the anterior hilum.
You can see the surgeon also using the fenestrated forceps in their other hands to blunt dissect around the vessel.
These instruments provide tactile feedback making this efficient but safe.
We have now gone around the inferior pulmonary vein with the fenestrated forceps preparing the path for stapling.
So now the other great advantage is that the surgeon is scrubbed so they can do their own stapling as opposed to relying on their assistant as in robotic cases.
Our standard practice, as in this case, is to introduce the stapler through a subziphoid port.
